front: Under Fourth Amendment abandonment doctrine, what does voluntarily abandoning property do to a person's ability to challenge its later search?
back: It eliminates any reasonable expectation of privacy in the property, so the person has no standing to challenge the search or seizure.

front: Why is abandonment analyzed as a standing question rather than as a search-warrant question?
back: Because if there is no retained reasonable expectation of privacy, the Fourth Amendment is not implicated at all and the defendant lacks standing to object — the search never had to be justified.

front: In Fourth Amendment abandonment analysis, what is the relationship between holding title to property and retaining a Fourth Amendment interest in it?
back: They are independent: it turns on the Fourth Amendment expectation-of-privacy standard, not state property law — a person can keep title yet abandon any Fourth Amendment interest.

front: What is the controlling question for whether property has been abandoned for Fourth Amendment purposes?
back: Whether the person retained an expectation of privacy "that society accepts as objectively reasonable" — the Greenwood/Katz standard.

front: What four factors do courts commonly weigh, as a synthesis of the case law, in the abandonment totality inquiry?
back: (1) denial of ownership, (2) physical relinquishment or discard, (3) the location where the item was left, and (4) intent inferred from conduct.

front: What is the proper status of the abandonment factors (denial, discard, location, intent) in the analysis?
back: They are not a checklist or independent test that must each be satisfied — they are factors bearing on the single ultimate question of whether a reasonable expectation of privacy was retained under Greenwood.

front: What did the Supreme Court hold in California v. Greenwood about garbage left for collection at the curb?
back: There is no reasonable expectation of privacy in garbage bags left for collection at the curb outside the curtilage, so a warrantless search and seizure of curbside trash does not violate the Fourth Amendment.

front: Which Supreme Court case held that police could search opaque garbage bags a suspect left at the curb for the trash collector without a warrant?
back: California v. Greenwood, 486 U.S. 35 (1988).

front: Greenwood's reason curbside trash fails the test: plastic garbage bags left on or at the side of a public street are "readily accessible to {{animals, children, scavengers, snoops, and other members of the public}}."
back: animals, children, scavengers, snoops, and other members of the public

front: What express location limit did Greenwood place on its curbside-trash holding?
back: The bags had to be left for collection "outside the curtilage of a home"; trash still within the curtilage is a different question not covered by Greenwood on its terms.

front: Common pitfall: why is it wrong to treat all of a suspect's trash as fair game under Greenwood?
back: Greenwood authorizes only the curbside bag left for collection outside the curtilage; trash sitting within the curtilage (e.g., a can beside the back door) is not covered.

front: What did Hester v. United States hold about containers a fleeing suspect dropped while running from officers?
back: By dropping the containers in flight the suspect abandoned any Fourth Amendment interest in them, so examining the contents was no seizure in the legal sense (abandonment by flight).
cite: Hester v. United States, 265 U.S. at 58

front: Hester (abandonment by flight): "there was no {{seizure in the sense of the law}} when the officers examined the contents of each after it had been abandoned."
back: seizure in the sense of the law
cite: Hester v. United States, 265 U.S. at 58

front: In which case did the defendant and an associate drop a jug, a jar, and a bottle while fleeing, and the Court treated the contents as abandoned?
back: Hester v. United States, 265 U.S. 57 (1924).

front: What did Abel v. United States hold about items a guest left in a hotel-room wastebasket?
back: Items left in the wastebasket after the guest had paid his bill and vacated the room were abandoned ("bona vacantia"), so the warrantless search and seizure was lawful.
cite: Abel v. United States, 362 U.S. at 241

front: In Abel, what fact marked the line that made the wastebasket items abandoned — mere absence from the room?
back: No — check-out, not mere absence. The guest had "paid his bill and vacated the room," giving the hotel the exclusive right to possession.
cite: Abel v. United States, 362 U.S. at 241

front: In Abel, what two grounds independently justified the warrantless search after the guest checked out?
back: The guest's abandonment of the articles, and the hotel's consent — once he vacated, the hotel had the exclusive right to possession.
cite: Abel v. United States, 362 U.S. at 241

front: What did Bond v. United States hold about a Border Patrol agent's physical squeezing of a bus passenger's carry-on bag?
back: The passenger retained a reasonable expectation of privacy in the bag, and the agent's exploratory physical manipulation (squeezing) was a Fourth Amendment search.
cite: Bond v. United States, 529 U.S. at 335-39

front: Bond on why squeezing the bag was a search: "Physically invasive inspection is simply {{more intrusive than purely visual inspection}}."
back: more intrusive than purely visual inspection
cite: Bond v. United States, 529 U.S. at 337

front: Which case held that an agent's exploratory squeezing of a bus passenger's overhead carry-on luggage was a search because a bailment is not abandonment?
back: Bond v. United States, 529 U.S. 334 (2000).

front: Bailment (in the abandonment context)
back: A temporary transfer of possession — e.g., handing a bag to a carrier, hotel, or friend — that preserves a privacy interest and does NOT abandon it.

front: Distinguish abandonment from bailment under the Fourth Amendment.
back: Abandonment relinquishes both possession and any reasonable expectation of privacy (no standing); a bailment relinquishes only possession temporarily and keeps the privacy interest intact (Bond).

front: Common pitfall: why is giving up possession not the same as giving up privacy?
back: A bailment surrenders possession temporarily but keeps a privacy interest, so it is not abandonment — e.g., a bag handed to a bus or luggage to a hotel (Bond).

front: What did United States v. Matlock hold about consent by a third party with common authority?
back: A third party with common authority over premises or effects may give valid consent to a search against an absent, nonconsenting co-occupant.
cite: United States v. Matlock, 415 U.S. at 171

front: "Common authority" under United States v. Matlock
back: Authority resting on "mutual use of the property by persons generally having joint access or control for most purposes" — not implied from a mere property interest.
cite: United States v. Matlock, 415 U.S. at 171 n.7

front: Under Matlock, what does common authority NOT rest on, and what does it rest on instead?
back: It does not rest on a property interest — common authority "is, of course, not to be implied from the mere property interest a third party has in the property"; it rests on mutual use and joint access or control.
cite: United States v. Matlock, 415 U.S. at 171 n.7

front: How does the common-authority route of Matlock relate to abandonment?
back: It is a separate third-party consent route, not a sub-test of abandonment: where there is no abandonment, a search may still be valid if someone with common authority consents.

front: Distinguish abandonment from consent as Fourth Amendment doctrines.
back: Abandonment means NO reasonable expectation of privacy exists at all, so the defendant lacks standing to object; consent presupposes a privacy interest exists but has been voluntarily waived. Different doctrines, different proof.

front: On the standing throughline, contrast abandonment, bailment, and common-authority consent as to whether a reasonable expectation of privacy survives.
back: Abandonment: no REP survives (no standing). Bailment: REP survives (Bond). Common-authority consent: REP survives but a third party with joint access/control validly waives it (Matlock).

front: What is the companion seizure-timing rule (California v. Hodari D.) for contraband discarded during flight?
back: Contraband a suspect tosses away while fleeing, before submitting to authority, is abandoned and admissible because no seizure of the person has yet occurred.
cite: California v. Hodari D., 499 U.S. at 623, 629

front: How do Hester and Hodari D. fit together on abandonment by flight?
back: Hester supplies the abandonment rule for items dropped in flight; Hodari D. supplies the companion seizure-of-the-person timing — the suspect who "tossed away" crack before submitting had abandoned it.
cite: California v. Hodari D., 499 U.S. at 629

front: Practical-capture maxim: how should an officer frame the question in the field to establish abandonment?
back: Ask whether the suspect has "anything to do with" the item — not merely whether it is "theirs." A clean disclaimer of any connection supports the inference that no expectation of privacy was retained.

front: Why does abandonment doctrine exist — what is its core analytical payoff for the Fourth Amendment?
back: Because privacy, not property, defines the Amendment's reach: once a person no longer holds an objectively reasonable expectation of privacy in an item, the government's examination of it is not a search or seizure at all.
